1: petach Levanon daltoteycha ve’tochal esh be’arazeycha: ‘Open your doors, Lebanon, so that fire may devour your cedars!’ The oracle against Lebanon’s forests is a poetic announcement of imminent destruction: the great cedar forests of Lebanon (a symbol of prestige and power throughout the ancient Near East — Solomon used Lebanese cedar for the temple, 1 Kgs 5) will be consumed by fire. The ironic command to open the doors (letting the destroying fire in) is the prophetic rhetoric of helplessness before divine judgment. The pastoral images of destroyed trees (cedars, cypresses, oaks of Bashan) in Zechariah 11:1-3 frame the allegory of the failed shepherd that follows.
2: heilil berosh ki nafal erez asher adarim shudadu heililu allonei Bashan ki yarad ya’ar habatzur: ‘Wail, cypress tree, for the cedar has fallen; the majestic trees are destroyed! Wail, oaks of Bashan, for the dense forest has fallen.’ The lamentation of trees over other trees is the prosopopoeia (personification) that characterizes prophetic poetry: the natural world mourns the destruction of the great ones (cedars = imperial powers; oaks of Bashan = regional powers). The descending order — Lebanon cedar, cypress, Bashan oak — traces the collapse of power from the greatest to the lesser. This imagery is the background for Revelation’s destruction of “every tree” (Revelation 8:7) as eschatological judgment.
3: qol yelalat haro’im ki shudad addaretam qol sha’agat keppirim ki shudad ge’on haYarden: ‘Listen to the cry of the shepherds — their splendor is destroyed! Listen to the roar of the young lions — the pride of the Jordan is ruined.’ The shepherds’ cry and the lions’ roar together frame the coming allegory: the rulers (shepherds) and the powerful (young lions) of the region will all be destroyed. The pride of the Jordan (ge’on haYarden) is the Jordan’s thicket — the dense vegetation along the riverbank where lions lived (Jeremiah 49:19; Zechariah 50:44) — representing natural power and abundance now stripped away. The transition from forest imagery to the shepherd allegory (Zechariah 11:4ff.) is smooth: the forest is the context, the shepherds are the actors.
4: koh amar YHWH Elohai re’eh et tzon ha’haregah: ‘This is what the LORD my God says: Shepherd the flock marked for slaughter.’ Tzon ha’haregah (flock of slaughter/flock destined for killing) — the flock is not merely at risk but has been designated for destruction by its own owners. This is the shocking premise of the allegory: the people are being treated as livestock for slaughter rather than as a flock to be protected. The prophet is commanded to act as a good shepherd for this abandoned, exploited flock — an enacted metaphor for YHWH’s own faithful shepherding of his people despite their leaders’ exploitation.
5: asher qoneihen yehargum velo ye’shamu umochreihem yomru baruch YHWH va’ashar: ‘Their buyers slaughter them without guilt. Those who sell them say, “Praise the LORD — I have become rich!” And their shepherds have no compassion on them.’ The buyers and sellers of the flock-people (the exploiting leadership) are portrayed in stark moral terms: the buyers kill without guilt (lo ye’shamu, no guilt felt); the sellers even invoke YHWH’s name to celebrate their profit. The use of baruch YHWH (blessed/praised be YHWH) in the context of exploitation is the OT’s most pointed example of religiosity weaponized for self-enrichment — the exact target of Amos 8:4-6 and Jesus’s condemnation of those who “devour widows’ houses and for a pretense make long prayers” (Mark 12:40).
6: ki lo achus od al yoshvei ha’aretz ne’um YHWH vehineni mematzzi et ha’adam: ‘For I will no longer spare the inhabitants of the land, declares the LORD. I am about to hand each person over to his neighbor and to his king.’ Lo achus (I will no longer spare/have compassion) — the withdrawal of divine protection, allowing the natural consequences of human exploitation and political chaos to play out. The phrase mematzzi et ha’adam ish beyad re’ehu uveyad malko (handing each person over to his neighbor and his king) describes social and political breakdown: when YHWH withdraws his protection, the community fragments into mutual exploitation. This is the darkest form of divine judgment — not active punishment but the withdrawal of the restraining grace that kept human evil in check.
7: ve’er’eh et tzon ha’haregah lachen ani aniyei hatzon va’eqqach li shnei maqelot le’achad qarati No’am uvele’achad qarati Chovlim va’er’eh et hatzon: ‘So I shepherded the flock marked for slaughter — the flock the dealers exploited. I took two staffs, calling one Favor and the other Union, and I shepherded the flock.’ The two staffs name the two dimensions of YHWH’s covenant relationship: No’am (Favor/Grace/Beauty/Pleasant) represents YHWH’s gracious covenantal relationship with his people; Chovlim (Union/Binders) represents the internal unity between Judah and Israel. Both are eventually broken in the allegory (Zechariah 11:10, 14), signifying the ending of both dimensions — divine favor and national unity.
8: va’achid shlosha haro’im biyerach echad vatiqtzar nafshi bahem vegam nafshtam ba’ala bi: ‘In one month I did away with three shepherds. But I grew impatient with them, and they loathed me in return.’ The three shepherds dismissed in one month are among the most debated references in Zechariah — no consensus identification has emerged. Possibilities include three leadership offices (prophet, priest, king), three Hasmonean rulers, three classes of foreign rulers, or a generic reference to the rapid succession of failed leaders. The mutual loathing (vatiqtzar nafshi bahem ... nafshtam ba’ala bi) — the prophet impatient with the shepherds, the shepherds loathing the prophet — reflects the fundamental breakdown of leadership accountability that characterizes the period.
9: va’omar lo er’eh etchem hamemet yamat vehanichcadet tikavet vehannish’arot to’chalnah isha et besar re’utah: ‘So I said, “I will no longer shepherd you. What is dying, let it die; what is to be destroyed, let it be destroyed; and those who are left will devour each other.”’ The withdrawal of the shepherd — the declaration that no more care will be given — is the most severe form of divine judgment in the shepherd-metaphor. The three-part fate (dying die, destroyed destroyed, survivors eat each other) is the escalating horror of complete social breakdown. This is the judgment that falls when the divine shepherd withdraws, and it functions as the enacted prophecy of the siege of Jerusalem in 70 CE, where Josephus reports the internal conflicts of the besieged city.
10: va’eqqach et maqeli et No’am va’egda oto lehfer et beriti asher karati et kol ha’ammim: ‘Then I took my staff called Favor and broke it, canceling the covenant I had made with all the peoples.’ The breaking of the Favor staff cancels the covenant with “all the peoples” — interpreted as YHWH’s restraint of the nations from destroying Israel (a protective covenant). When this protective covenant is revoked, the nations are free to exercise their destructive hostility toward Israel. The breaking of the staff is a prophetic sign-act of enormous gravity: the physical breaking enacts the theological reality of covenantal withdrawal.
11: vattufar bayom hahu vedeu ken aniyei hatzon ha’shomerim oti ki davar YHWH hu: ‘It was canceled on that day. The sheep merchants who were watching understood that this was the word of the LORD.’ The recognition by the aniyei hatzon ha’shomerim (the poor/afflicted ones of the flock who were watching) is significant — it is specifically the humble, attentive ones who recognize the prophetic sign-act as the word of YHWH. Those who recognized the shepherd’s departure as divinely significant are the OT type of those who recognized Jesus as the fulfillment of the prophetic shepherd pattern.
12: va’omar alehem im tov be’eineichem havu sechari ve’im lo chadalu va’ishqelu et sechari sheloshim keseph: ‘I said to them, “If you think it right, pay me my wages; if not, keep them.” So they weighed out thirty pieces of silver.’ Sheloshim keseph (thirty pieces of silver) — the standard price for a slave (Exodus 21:32: if an ox gores a slave, the owner pays thirty shekels of silver). The amount is insulting for a shepherd’s wages — it is slave-compensation, not shepherd-pay. Matthew 26:15 and Zechariah 27:3-10 cite this directly: Judas received thirty pieces of silver for betraying Jesus; when he returned it and it was thrown into the temple, the priests used it to buy the potter’s field. Matthew attributes the quotation to Jeremiah (Zechariah 27:9) but the verbal echo of Zechariah 11:12-13 is unmistakable — a case of complex prophetic intertextuality.
13: va’yomer YHWH elai hashlichehu el hayotzer eder hayeqar asher yeqarti mealeihem va’eqqach et sheloshim ha’keseph va’ashlich oto beit YHWH el hayotzer: ‘But the LORD said to me, “Throw it to the potter” — this magnificent sum at which they valued me. So I took the thirty pieces of silver and threw them into the house of the LORD to the potter.’ The sarcastic phrase eder hayeqar asher yeqarti mealeihem (this magnificent/glorious price at which they valued me) drips with irony: thirty silver pieces as the “magnificence” of their assessment. The word yotzer (potter) and otzar (treasury) are acoustically similar in Hebrew, which may explain the LXX’s reading “into the treasury” and Matthew’s use of both words (Matthew 27:6: thrown into the treasury; Zechariah 27:10: given for the potter’s field). The potter figure also echoes Jeremiah 18-19 (the potter’s house as a place of divine reshaping and judgment).
14: va’egdod et maqeli ha’sheni et Chovlim lehfer et ha’achavah bein Yehudah uvein Yisrael: ‘Then I broke my second staff called Union, canceling the bond of brotherhood between Judah and Israel.’ The breaking of the second staff — Chovlim (Union, the bond of brotherhood between Judah and Israel) — is the prophetic enactment of the final rupture between the northern and southern peoples. The “bond of brotherhood” (ha’achavah) was already severed historically in 931 BCE and never fully healed; this breaking declares the theological cancellation of the covenantal unity that YHWH had maintained despite the political division. The restoration passages (Ezekiel 37:15-28: the two sticks joined; Zechariah 10:6-12: Judah and Joseph gathered together) represent the eschatological reversal of this broken union.
15: The second prophetic sign-act: YHWH commands the prophet to take the implements of a foolish shepherd. The contrast with the faithful shepherd of Zechariah 11:4-14 is now explicit: after the rejected shepherd comes the foolish (ewil) shepherd. The sign-act models the coming of a leadership antithetical to YHWH’s purpose — a shepherd who will prove worthless. The sequence of faithful shepherd rejected → foolish shepherd installed is the OT pattern for what happens when good leadership is despised: it is replaced by harmful leadership. This is the structural basis for the NT’s contrast between Christ (the good shepherd, John 10:11) and the antichrist-shepherd (the man of lawlessness, 2 Thessalonians 2:3-4).
16: ki hineni maqim ro’eh ba’aretz hanichladot lo yifqod hanno’arot lo yevaqesh uvehannivkeret lo yerape: ‘For I am about to raise up a shepherd in the land who will not care for those that are perishing, or seek the young, or heal the injured.’ The anti-shepherd is defined by a series of negatives: will not visit the perishing (lo yifqod — not performing the visitation that is YHWH’s characteristic action), not seek the straying, not heal the broken, not feed the healthy. He will instead eat the flesh of the fat sheep and tear off their hooves — exploitative, not protective. This is the anti-type of Christ’s shepherd ministry: Jesus heals the injured (the blind, lame, sick throughout the Gospels), seeks the straying (Luke 15:4), feeds the healthy (the feeding of the 5,000), and gives his life rather than consuming the flock.
17: hoy ro’i ha’elil ozoev hatzon cherev al zero’o ve’al ein yemino: ‘Woe to the worthless shepherd who abandons the flock! May a sword strike his arm and his right eye! Let his arm be completely withered, his right eye totally blinded!’ The ro’i ha’elil (worthless/idol shepherd — elil meaning both “worthless” and “idol/nothing”) who abandons the flock receives the woe-curse: arm withered and eye blinded — the two primary tools of the shepherd (arm for strength, eye for watching). The arm that should protect is disabled; the eye that should guard is blinded. The anti-shepherd is judicially crippled in the organs that corresponded to his pastoral responsibility — the punishment fitting the crime of abandonment.
